explanation_md: `Planet::earth` is the third enumerator (0-indexed: mercury=0, venus=1, earth=2), so `static_cast<int>(p)` produces `2`. The `operator<<` overload prints `"Earth"` for `Planet::earth`. Both lines end with `'\n'`.

options.b: Line 8: you cannot compare enum class values with `==`
options.c: Line 3: `enum class` requires an underlying type like `enum class Size : int`
options.d: Line 7: `Size s` should be `Size s = Size::small` first, then assigned

explanation_md: Scoped enumerators are not visible outside the enum's scope, so bare `medium` is undeclared. The fix is `Size s{ Size::medium };`. Options (b) and (c) describe features that work fine by default; option (d) describes unnecessary gymnastics.

prompt_md: Which statement best describes the difference between `enum` and `enum class`?

options.a: `enum class` enumerators must be assigned explicit integer values; plain `enum` assigns them automatically
options.b: `enum class` enumerators are scoped to the enum name and do not implicitly convert to `int`; plain `enum` enumerators pollute the enclosing scope and convert silently
options.c: `enum class` is only available in C++20 and later; plain `enum` works in all versions
options.d: There is no practical difference; `enum class` is just a style alias

explanation_md: The two defining differences are name scoping (you must write `Color::red` for a scoped enum) and the absence of implicit integer conversion (you need `static_cast<int>` to get the underlying value). Both features were introduced in C++11. The other options are incorrect.
`Flag::write` was explicitly assigned the value `2`. `static_cast<int>(f)` converts the scoped enumerator to its underlying integer, which is `2`.
